title: Short tester video of my Omega Gold Pro EA in action – pyramiding through a strong gold uptrend with real risk control
author: Loose-Object-8913
selftext:
I’ve been trading gold manually for about 6 years and recently decided to turn my strategy into an automated system.

At the start I was obsessed with trying to improve the entries. I kept thinking if I could just find the “perfect signal” the results would improve. Actually drove me insane.

But after running a lot of tests I realised something else mattered way more… how the system manages risk once it’s already in the trade.

Things like stop placement, volatility filters, position sizing, and controlled pyramiding actually had a bigger impact on the equity curve than tweaking the entry logic.

It kind of flipped the way I was thinking about strategy design.

So I recorded this short video straight from the tester to show exactly that in action:

You’ll see it catch a clean H4 uptrend on XAUUSD, enter on the H1 breakout, then start adding positions as price keeps running — all while ATR stops, equity protection, and proper % risk sizing keep everything controlled. No blow-ups, just the pyramiding working the way I designed it.

MQL5 - https://www.mql5.com/en/market/product/168039

YouTube - https://youtu.be/Q0jbBDaYQaY?si=KBewsenDcM\_2Cl5V

Curious if anyone else who’s built systems ran into the same thing — did you find risk management mattered more than the signal itself? And what do you think of how the pyramiding looks in the video? Does it feel balanced for live trading?

Not hyping, just sharing what finally clicked for me after all the iterations.

Cheers

(Dev behind Omega Gold Pro)

comments.author: Patient_Kangaroo4864
comments.body:
Totally agree with the takeaway that risk management matters more than “perfect” entries — that’s a lesson most of us only learn after way too many iterations.

A couple of things I’d be curious about, especially with pyramiding on gold:

- How are you handling regime changes? Strong uptrends are great for pyramiding, but gold can flip to mean-reverting chop fast. Is there a volatility or trend-strength filter that actually turns the system *off*?
- What’s the max heat / worst historical DD during pyramiding sequences? That tends to be the silent killer even when individual trades are well sized.
- Are stops static (ATR-based, structure-based) or do they evolve as positions stack?
- Have you looked at performance around high-impact news (CPI, FOMC)? Gold EAs often look clean until slippage and spreads widen.

Not trying to nitpick — just genuinely interested, since most automated gold systems die not because of entries, but because pyramiding + volatility expansion eventually collides with risk limits. If you’ve managed to solve *that* part robustly, that’s the real achievement.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
Good questions actually.

The regime change problem on gold was one of the biggest headaches when I was testing this. Strong trends are great for pyramiding but gold can suddenly turn into messy range conditions. The EA uses a mix of trend and volatility filters and if things start looking too choppy it just stops opening new trades and waits.

For pyramiding I’m not stacking full size trades. Each position added is smaller so the overall exposure stays controlled. That was important because like you said, pyramiding can blow things up fast if you’re not careful.

Stops are ATR based rather than fixed so they adapt to volatility, and once a move gets going the trailing logic starts locking in profit.

I also try to avoid the obvious high impact news stuff (CPI, FOMC etc). Gold spreads can get pretty wild during those.

Still testing and refining things though. The biggest lesson for me while building this was that risk management mattered way more than trying to find the “perfect” entry.

comments.author: dogazine4570
comments.body:
Really like that you highlighted the shift from “perfect entry” to risk management. A lot of traders (myself included) waste years tweaking signals when the real edge often comes from how you handle exposure after entry.

With pyramiding in a strong gold trend, I’d be curious how you’re controlling compounding risk — are you reducing incremental position size as volatility expands, or trailing the entire basket based on blended BE? Gold can snap back hard, especially around news.

Also interested in how you’re filtering regime changes. Do you use a volatility contraction/expansion model or higher‑TF structure to decide when pyramiding is allowed vs. when it’s capped?

If you’re comfortable sharing, some stats like max DD during trend exhaustion phases would add a lot of context. Risk control > entry precision is a lesson most people learn the hard way.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
Yeah that’s something I had to think about a lot while testing.

I’m not stacking full size positions when pyramiding. Each additional trade is smaller so the total exposure doesn’t get out of hand if the move suddenly reverses.

Stops are ATR based so they adapt to volatility, and once the move starts developing the trailing logic starts protecting the basket rather than just individual trades.

Gold definitely snaps back hard sometimes so controlling that compounding risk was one of the bigger things I focused on.

For regime changes I’m mostly using higher-TF trend and volatility filters. If the market starts looking more like chop than trend the EA just stops opening new positions.

Still refining and testing things though. The biggest thing I learned building it was that risk control mattered way more than trying to find a perfect entry signal.

comments.author: Loose-Object-8913
comments.body:
Yeah it’s actually not trading right now. No clean setup so it’s just sitting out 😅
That was a big thing I added, not forcing trades in messy conditions (learned that the hard way lol).
The video is from the teser when it caught a nice move.
Happy to answer anything 👍
